tweets.title: US annouces 30-day wavier to get Russian oil stranded in sea for "vulnerable nations:
tweets.content:
US annouces 30-day wavier to get Russian oil stranded in sea for "vulnerable nations:

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent (@SecScottBessent)

.@USTreasury is issuing a temporary 30-day general license to provide the most vulnerable nations with the ability to temporarily access Russian oil currently stranded at sea.

This extension will provide additional flexibility, and we will work with these nations to provide specific licenses as needed. This general license will help stabilize the physical crude market and ensure oil reaches the most energy-vulnerable countries.

It will also help reroute existing supply to countries most in need by reducing China’s ability to stockpile discounted oil.

Arunachal Pradesh Governor K T Parnaik on Monday stressed the need for strengthening border management, infrastructure and security preparedness in frontier areas during a meeting with Chief of Defence Staff General Anil Chauhan in the national capital.

They discussed a range of national security issues concerning Arunachal Pradesh, with a focus on security along the international border, development of frontier villages and implementation of welfare-oriented projects in remote areas. 

Governor Parnaik appreciated the role of the armed forces in maintaining close coordination with the state government and said the strong civil-military partnership in Arunachal Pradesh has played a significant role in ensuring both security and development in border villages.

In a remarkable turn of events that marks one of the most dramatic comebacks in modern Indian military history, Brigadier Shrikant Prasad Purohit has assumed charge as Brigadier GS (Intelligence) at Headquarters South Western Command, Jaipur.

Once at the center of the 2008 Malegaon blast case, Purohit spent nearly nine years in jail and fought a legal battle that lasted close to 18 years. Throughout the ordeal, he maintained that his interactions with extremist elements were part of authorized Military Intelligence operations aimed at infiltrating and monitoring suspicious networks.

In July 2025, the NIA Special Court acquitted him and all other accused, concluding that the prosecution failed to prove the charges beyond reasonable doubt. Following his acquittal, the Indian Army restored his career progression, promoted him to Brigadier, and entrusted him with one of the most sensitive intelligence appointments in the force.

As Brigadier GS (Intelligence) at South Western Command, headquartered in Jaipur, he will oversee strategic intelligence assessments related to the western theatre, including Rajasthan’s key border districts such as Bikaner, Sri Ganganagar, Jaisalmer, Barmer, and adjoining sectors linked to Punjab and Gujarat. His role will involve monitoring Pakistani military deployments, drone intrusions, cross-border smuggling routes, terror networks, and emerging hybrid warfare threats.

For many in the defence community, this appointment symbolizes institutional confidence, professional vindication, and the extraordinary resilience of an officer who returned from one of the most controversial legal battles in India to occupy a coveted Military Intelligence position at the heart of the nation’s western security architecture.

In a remarkable turn of events that marks one of the most dramatic comebacks in modern Indian military history, Brigadier Shrikant Prasad Purohit has assumed charge as Brigadier GS (Intelligence) at Headquarters South Western Command, Jaipur.

Once at the center of the 2008 Malegaon blast case, Purohit spent nearly nine years in jail and fought a legal battle that lasted close to 18 years. Throughout the ordeal, he maintained that his interactions with extremist elements were part of authorized Military Intelligence operations aimed at infiltrating and monitoring suspicious networks.

In July 2025, the NIA Special Court acquitted him and all other accused, concluding that the prosecution failed to prove the charges beyond reasonable doubt. Following his acquittal, the Indian Army restored his career progression, promoted him to Brigadier, and entrusted him with one of the most sensitive intelligence appointments in the force.

As Brigadier GS (Intelligence) at South Western Command, headquartered in Jaipur, he will oversee strategic intelligence assessments related to the western theatre, including Rajasthan’s key border districts such as Bikaner, Sri Ganganagar, Jaisalmer, Barmer, and adjoining sectors linked to Punjab and Gujarat. His role will involve monitoring Pakistani military deployments, drone intrusions, cross-border smuggling routes, terror networks, and emerging hybrid warfare threats.

For many in the defence community, this appointment symbolizes institutional confidence, professional vindication, and the extraordinary resilience of an officer who returned from one of the most controversial legal battles in India to occupy a coveted Military Intelligence position at the heart of the nation’s western security architecture.

R to @NetramDefence: More widely, the UAE and India share a deep and enduring relationship underpinned by a Comprehensive Strategic Partnership that has expanded significantly in recent years across trade, energy and infrastructure. 

In 2022, the UAE and India signed a Comprehensive Economic Partnership Agreement, which has led to a 14.7 % increase in trade activity in 2025 according to data from the UAE government. 

These developments build on the momentum generated by earlier agreements and growing economic integration between the two countries. Bilateral ties have strengthened significantly in recent years, supported by major investments—including a $5 billion UAE investment package and long-term energy contracts, with both countries targeting $200 billion in trade by 2032.
